name: JMC 126 - Learning, Teaching and Applying Geometry and Handling Data

questions.question: What is the primary object of elementary geometry?
options: To determine the properties of geometric figures by a definite process of reasoning
questions.explanation: The module states: 'The primary object of elementary geometry is to determine, by a definite process of reasoning that will be introduced and developed later, the properties of geometric figures.' (Unit 1, Introduction)

questions.question: What are the fundamental principles in elementary geometry called?
questions.explanation: The module: 'In elementary geometry these fundamental principles are called definitions and assumptions. The assumptions here mentioned are divided into two classes, axioms and postulates.' (Unit 1, Introduction)

questions.question: What is a geometric figure?
options: A point, line, surface, or solid, or a combination of any or all of these
questions.explanation: The module: 'A geometric figure is a point, line, surface, or solid, or a combination of any or all of these.' (Unit 1, Introduction)

questions.question: Two geometric figures are said to coincide if:
options: When either is placed upon the other, each point of one lies upon some point of the other
They have the same number of sides
questions.explanation: The module: 'Two geometric figures are said to coincide if, when either is placed upon the other, each point of one lies upon some point of the other.' (Unit 1, Introduction)

questions.question: Two geometric figures are equal if:
options: They can be made to coincide
questions.explanation: The module: 'Two geometric figures are equal if they can be made to coincide.' (Unit 1, Introduction)

questions.question: What is superposition in geometry?
options: The process of placing one figure upon another so that the two shall coincide
The process of adding two figures
The process of subtracting two figures
The process of measuring a figure
questions.explanation: The module: 'The process of placing one figure upon another so that the two shall coincide is called superposition.' (Unit 1, Introduction)

questions.question: What is a straight line?
options: A line such that, if any portion of it is placed with its ends in the line, the entire portion so placed will lie in the line
A line that has no end
A line that is made of many points
questions.explanation: The module: 'A straight line is a line such that, if any portion of it is placed with its ends in the line, the entire portion so placed will lie in the line, however it may be applied.' (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: A straight line is also called a:
questions.explanation: The module: 'A straight line is also called a right line.' (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What is an angle?
options: The figure formed by two straight lines which diverge from a point or converge to a point
questions.explanation: The module: 'An angle is the figure formed by two straight lines which diverge from a point or converge to a point.' (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: In an angle, what is the vertex?
options: The point where the two lines meet
questions.explanation: The module: 'The point is the vertex of the angle and the lines are its sides (arms of the angles).' (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: Two angles are adjacent if:
options: They have a common vertex and a common side between them
questions.explanation: The module: 'Two angles are adjacent if they have a common vertex and a common side between them.' (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: How is a right angle defined?
options: It is one-quarter of a whole turn and is equal to 90°
questions.explanation: The module: 'Right angle: It is one-quarter of a whole turn and is the same as the angle made by a horizontal line and vertical line. The measure of the angle is 90°.' (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What is an acute angle?
options: An angle less than a right angle (less than 90°)
questions.explanation: The module: 'An acute angle is an angle that is less than a right angle.' (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What is an obtuse angle?
options: An angle greater than a right angle but less than two right angles (between 90° and 180°)
questions.explanation: The module: 'An obtuse angle is an angle that is greater than right-angle but less than two right-angles.' (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a reflex angle?
options: An angle greater than two right-angles but less than four right-angles (between 180° and 360°)
questions.explanation: The module: 'a reflex angle is an angle that is greater than two right-angles but less than four right-angles.' (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What are complementary angles?
options: Two angles whose sum is a right angle (90°)
questions.explanation: The module: 'angles 1 and 2 are called complementary angles (complementary angles sum up to 90°).' (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What are supplementary angles?
options: Two angles whose sum is two right angles (180°)
questions.explanation: The module: 'angles 3 and 4 are called supplementary angles (supplementary angles sum up to 180°).' (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What are vertically opposite angles?
options: Two angles whose sides are extensions of each other; they are equal
questions.explanation: The module: 'Two angles are said to be vertically opposite if the sides of each are extended. ... angles a and c are vertically opposite angles, and angles b and d are likewise vertically opposite angles.' (Unit 1, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the sum of angles at a point?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Angles at a point add up to 360°.' (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What are parallel lines?
options: Two lines in a plane which do not meet no matter how far they are extended
Two lines that meet at a point
questions.explanation: The module: 'Parallel lines are two lines in a plane which do not meet no matter how far they are extended.' (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a transversal line?
options: A line which cuts a pair of lines
A line that is parallel to another
A line that is perpendicular to another
A line that meets another at a point
questions.explanation: The module: 'A transversal line is a line which cuts a pair of lines.' (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What are corresponding angles?
options: Angles in the same or corresponding positions at the point of intersection of the transversal line
questions.explanation: The module: 'The angles b and g are called corresponding angles because they are in the same or corresponding positions at the point of intersection of the transversal line.' (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the property of corresponding angles?
questions.explanation: The module: 'We conclude that corresponding angles are equal.' (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What are alternate angles?
options: Angles on opposite sides of the transversal and between the parallel lines
questions.explanation: The module: 'The angles b and f are called alternate angles. Also <c and <e are alternate angles.' (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the property of alternate angles?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Alternate angles are equal.' (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What are co-interior angles?
options: Angles on the same side of the transversal and between the parallel lines
questions.explanation: The module: 'The angles b and e are called co-interior angles. Angles c and f are also co-interior angles.' (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the property of co-interior angles?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Their property is that they sum up to 180°.' (Unit 1, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a triangle?
options: A simple closed curve created by three line-segments
questions.explanation: The module: 'A triangle is a simple closed curve which is created by three line-segments.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the sum of the interior angles of a triangle?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The sum of the interior angles is always 180°.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the relationship between an exterior angle of a triangle and the interior angles?
options: The exterior angle is equal to the sum of the two opposite interior angles
The exterior angle is equal to the sum of all interior angles
The exterior angle is supplementary to the interior angle
The exterior angle is equal to the interior angle
questions.explanation: The module: 'We can also conclude that the exterior angle of triangle is equal to the sum of the two opposite interior angles.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is an equilateral triangle?
options: A triangle with all sides equal and all interior angles equal (60°)
A triangle with two equal sides
A triangle with no equal sides
A triangle with one right angle
questions.explanation: The module: 'An equilateral triangle has the following properties: All sides are equal in length. All interior angles are equal, that is 60°.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is an isosceles triangle?
options: A triangle with two equal sides and two equal interior angles
A triangle with all sides equal
A triangle with no equal sides
A triangle with one right angle
questions.explanation: The module: 'The properties of an isosceles triangle are: Two of its sides are equal; Two of its interior angles are equal.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is a scalene triangle?
options: A triangle with all sides different and all interior angles different
A triangle with two equal sides
A triangle with all sides equal
A triangle with one right angle
questions.explanation: The module: 'A scalene triangle has all its sides being different in length and all interior angles are different.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is an obtuse-angled triangle?
options: A triangle with one interior angle more than 90°
A triangle with all angles acute
A triangle with one right angle
A triangle with all angles equal
questions.explanation: The module: 'This is a triangle with one of its interior angles more than 90°.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is an acute-angled triangle?
options: A triangle with one of its interior angles being less than 90°
A triangle with all angles acute
A triangle with one right angle
A triangle with one obtuse angle
questions.explanation: The module defines: 'Acute angled Triangle: A triangle with one of its interior angles being less than 90°.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is a right-angled triangle?
options: A triangle with one interior angle equal to 90°
A triangle with all angles equal
A triangle with one obtuse angle
A triangle with all angles acute
questions.explanation: The module: 'A right angled triangle has one interior angle being equal to 90°.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is a quadrilateral?
options: A polygon with four edges and four vertices
questions.explanation: The module: 'a quadrilateral is a polygon with four edges (or sides) and four vertices or corners.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the sum of the interior angles of a simple quadrilateral?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The interior angles of a simple (and planar) quadrilateral ABCD add up to 360 degrees.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a rectangle?
options: A quadrilateral with opposite sides equal and each angle 90°
A quadrilateral with all sides equal
A quadrilateral with one pair of parallel sides
A quadrilateral with no parallel sides
questions.explanation: The module: 'A rectangle is a quadrilateral with opposite sides equal and each angle is 90°.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a square?
options: A quadrilateral with all sides equal and each angle 90°
A quadrilateral with opposite sides equal
A quadrilateral with one pair of parallel sides
A quadrilateral with no equal sides
questions.explanation: The module: 'It is a quadrilateral with all sides being equal and each angle is a right angle.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a rhombus?
options: A quadrilateral with all sides equal
A quadrilateral with opposite sides equal
A quadrilateral with one pair of parallel sides
A quadrilateral with no equal sides
questions.explanation: The module: 'Rhombus has all sides being equal.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a parallelogram?
options: A quadrilateral with opposite sides equal and parallel, opposite angles equal
A quadrilateral with all sides equal
A quadrilateral with one pair of parallel sides
A quadrilateral with no parallel sides
questions.explanation: The module: 'Parallelogram has the following properties: 1. Opposite angles are equal 2. Two pairs of opposite sides that are equal and parallel.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a kite?
options: A quadrilateral with two pairs of adjacent sides equal
A quadrilateral with all sides equal
A quadrilateral with opposite sides equal
A quadrilateral with one pair of parallel sides
questions.explanation: The module: 'Kite is a quadrilateral with two pairs of adjacent sides equal.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a trapezium?
options: A quadrilateral with one pair of parallel sides
A quadrilateral with two pairs of parallel sides
A quadrilateral with all sides equal
A quadrilateral with no parallel sides
questions.explanation: The module: 'A trapezium is a quadrilateral with one pair of parallel sides.' (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a polyhedron?
options: A solid made up of only polygonal faces, the faces meet at edges which are line segments, and the edges meet at points called vertices
A solid with only one face
questions.explanation: The module: 'A solid is a polyhedron if it is made up of only polygonal faces, the faces meet at edges which are line segments and the edges meet at points called vertices.' (Unit 1, Session 5)

questions.question: What is a cuboid?
options: A solid with rectangular faces, opposite faces equal and parallel
A solid with all faces square
questions.explanation: The module gives examples: 'Objects like the matchbox, fridge, system unit of a computer are all examples of cuboid.' (Unit 1, Session 5)

questions.question: What is a cylinder?
options: A solid with two circular bases and a curved surface
questions.explanation: The module gives examples: 'milk tin, milo tin, drum of oil' as examples of cylinders. (Unit 1, Session 5)

questions.question: What is a geometry net?
options: A two-dimensional shape that can be folded to form a three-dimensional shape
questions.explanation: The module: 'A geometry net is a two-dimensional shape that can be folded to form a three-dimensional shape or a solid.' (Unit 1, Session 5)

questions.question: What does Euler's formula state for solids?
questions.explanation: The module: 'For solids V + F = E + 2.' (Unit 1, Session 5)

questions.question: What are congruent triangles?
options: Triangles that have both the same shape and the same size
Triangles that have the same shape but not the same size
Triangles that have the same area
Triangles that have the same perimeter
questions.explanation: The module: 'Congruent triangles have both the same shape and the same size.' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: What are similar triangles?
options: Triangles that have the same shape, but not necessarily the same size
Triangles that have both the same shape and size
Triangles that have the same area
Triangles that have the same perimeter
questions.explanation: The module: 'Similar triangles have the same shape, but not necessarily the same size.' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: What is a line of symmetry?
options: A line that divides a figure into two identical halves that are mirror images
A line that divides a figure into two equal areas
A line that is perpendicular to a figure
A line that is parallel to a figure
questions.explanation: The module: 'The 'mirror' or 'fold' line that gives these two equal reflections is called the line of symmetry.' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: What is rotational symmetry?
options: When a figure can be rotated to make the same pattern again
When a figure can be flipped
When a figure has a line of symmetry
When a figure has no symmetry
questions.explanation: The module: 'the square also has rotational symmetry, meaning if we rotate it (turn it around) we can get the same pattern again.' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: How many lines of symmetry does a rectangle have?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The rectangle has two [lines of symmetry].' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: How many lines of symmetry does a square have?
questions.explanation: The module: 'the square has four [lines of symmetry].' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: What is a congruent triangle criterion based on sides?
options: All three pairs of corresponding sides are equal (SSS)
Two pairs of sides and the included angle are equal (SAS)
Two pairs of angles and the included side are equal (ASA)
All of the above are criteria
questions.explanation: The module lists criteria: 'All three pairs of corresponding sides are equal. Two pairs of corresponding sides and the corresponding angles between them are equal. Two pairs of corresponding angles and the corresponding sides between them are equal.' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: What is the difference between congruence and similarity?
options: Congruent figures have same shape and size; similar figures have same shape but different size
Congruent figures have same size only
Similar figures have same size only
questions.explanation: The module distinguishes: 'Congruent triangles have both the same shape and the same size. Similar triangles have the same shape, but not necessarily the same size.' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a square?
questions.explanation: The module: 'a square has a rotational symmetry of four.' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a rectangle?
questions.explanation: The module states the square has rotational symmetry of 4, and implies rectangle has order 2 (since it has two lines of symmetry and no other rotational symmetry). (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: What is the order of rotational symmetry of an equilateral triangle?
questions.explanation: The module: 'It has rotational symmetry of order 3' for equilateral triangle. (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is a solid?
options: A three-dimensional object that does not lie completely in a plane
questions.explanation: The module: '3-D shapes/objects are those which do not lie completely in a plane.' (Unit 1, Session 5)

questions.question: What are the basic elements of a triangle?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The basic elements of the triangle are sides, angles, and vertices.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is an interior angle of a triangle?
options: An angle formed by extending a side
questions.explanation: The module: 'An interior angle is an angle inside the shape.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is an exterior angle of a triangle?
options: An angle formed by extending one side of the triangle
questions.explanation: The module: 'The angles marked a, b, and c are called exterior angles because they are outside the triangle.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is a vertex of a solid?
options: The line where two faces meet
questions.explanation: The module: 'the edges meet at points called vertices.' (Unit 1, Session 5)

questions.question: What is an edge of a solid?
options: The line segment where two faces meet
questions.explanation: The module: 'the faces meet at edges which are line segments.' (Unit 1, Session 5)

questions.question: What is a face of a solid?
options: The line where two faces meet
questions.explanation: The module: 'Each surface of a solid is called face.' (Unit 1, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the first step in bisecting a straight line AB?
options: With the compass point at A, draw an arc above and below the line
With the compass point at B, draw an arc
questions.explanation: The module: 'i) Draw a straight line AB of any convenient length. ii) With the compass point at A and with any reasonable radius draw an arc above and below the line AB.' (Unit 2, Session 1)

questions.question: To construct a perpendicular at a point on a line, after drawing a semi-circle around the point, what is the next step?
options: With the compass point at one intersection, draw an arc on one side of the line
Draw a straight line from the point to the arc
With the same radius, draw an arc from the other intersection
questions.explanation: The module: 'iii) With compass point at P, and with a convenient radius, draw an arc on any one side of the line.' (Unit 2, Session 1)

questions.question: To construct a perpendicular from a point outside a line, after drawing arcs to cut the line at A and B, what is the next step?
options: Place the compass at A, draw an arc on the other side of the line
Draw a straight line from M to K
With B as centre, draw an arc to intersect the first arc
questions.explanation: The module: 'iii) Place the compass at point A, draw an arc on the other side of the line in the opposite side of m. iv) Using B as the centre and with the same radius as in iii) draw an arc to intersect that arc.' (Unit 2, Session 1)

questions.question: To construct a parallel line to a given line, after drawing semi-circles above the line at two points, what is the final step?
options: Draw a line to touch the turning points of the two semi-circles
Join the two points with a straight line
questions.explanation: The module: 'v) Draw a line to touch the turning points of the two semi-circles at R and S. vi) Line RS is parallel to line PQ.' (Unit 2, Session 1)

questions.question: To construct a 60° angle, after drawing a semi-circle around P, what is the next step?
options: Using the same radius and the compass point at X, draw an arc to intersect the first arc
Draw a line from P to the intersection
questions.explanation: The module: 'iii) Using the same radius and the compass point at X, draw an arc to intersect with the first arc at Y.' (Unit 2, Session 2)

questions.question: How do you construct a 30° angle?
options: First construct a 60° angle and then bisect it
First construct a 90° angle and then bisect it
First construct a 120° angle and then bisect it
First construct a 45° angle and then bisect it
questions.explanation: The module: 'To construct 30° we first have to construct 60° and thereafter bisect the 60° to get 30°.' (Unit 2, Session 2)

questions.question: To construct a 120° angle, after drawing a semi-circle around P, what is the next step?
options: Using the same radius, draw an arc from the first intersection point, then from the second intersection point, then join P to the final point
Draw a straight line from P to the first arc
Draw a line from P to the second arc
questions.explanation: The module: 'iii) With same radius as in (i) and N as the centre, draw an arc to cut the semi-circle at A. iv) With A as a centre and using the same radius as (ii) draw another arc to cut the semi-circle at V. v) Join P to V with a straight line to get 120°.' (Unit 2, Session 2)

questions.question: How do you construct a 90° angle?
options: Construct a semi-circle, then using the same radius draw arcs from the intersections, then from those points draw arcs to intersect above, then join to the point
Construct a 120° angle and bisect it
questions.explanation: The module describes a method: 'ii) With P as the centre... draw a semi-circle... iii) Using the same radius... draw arcs to cut the semi-circle at A and B. iv) Using same radius... with A and B as centres draw arcs to intersect above the semi-circle at V. v) Join V to P.' (Unit 2, Session 2)

questions.question: How do you construct a 135° angle?
options: Construct a 90° angle and a 45° angle, then combine them (90° + 45°)
questions.explanation: The module: 'Angle 135° is equal to 90° + 45°. To construct this we first construct 90°, bisect the other 90° to get 45° and 90° which will add up to 135°.' (Unit 2, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the first step in constructing a triangle given the length of all three sides?
options: Draw one side of the triangle
Draw an arc from one endpoint
Draw an arc from the other endpoint
Join the intersection to both endpoints
questions.explanation: The module: 'i) Construct AB of length say 6cm. ii) With A as the centre, and a radius of 6cm, draw an arc above the line AB. iii) Using the same radius as in (ii) and B as the centre, construct the arc to intersect with the first arc above AB at C. iv) Join A to C and B to C.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: When constructing a triangle given two sides and the included angle, what is the first step?
options: Draw one side, then construct the given angle at one end
questions.explanation: The module: 'i) Draw line PQ = 8cm. ii) Construct angle PQR = 135° at Q.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: When constructing a triangle given two angles and a side, what is the first step?
options: Draw the given side, then construct the two angles at their respective points to intersect
Construct one angle, then draw the side
questions.explanation: The module: 'i) Draw AB = 5cm. ii) Construct the two interior angles at their respective places or points to intersect at C.' (Unit 2, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the first step in constructing a square of side 7cm?
options: Construct perpendiculars at A and B
Open the compass to 7cm and draw arcs from A and B
questions.explanation: The module: 'i) Construct line AB = 7cm. ii) Construct perpendiculars at A and B. iii) Open the compass to 7cm radius and with the compass point at A, draw an arc to cut the perpendicular at D. iv) Using the same radius ... at B, draw an arc to cut the perpendicular at C. v) Join D to C.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the first step in constructing a rectangle with length 9cm and breadth 7cm?
options: Construct perpendiculars at A and B
Draw arcs from A and B with radius 7cm
questions.explanation: The module: 'i) Construct line AB = 9cm. ii) Construct perpendicular at A and B. iii) With the compass point at A, and a radius of 7cm, draw an arc to cut the perpendicular at D. iv) Using the same radius as in (iii), and the compass point at B, make an arc to cut the perpendicular at C. v) Join C to D.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the first step in constructing a parallelogram PQRS with PQ=6cm, PS=4cm, and angle SPQ=60°?
options: Draw an arc from P with radius 4cm
Draw arcs from Q and S
questions.explanation: The module: 'i) Draw line PQ of length 6cm. ii) Construct angle 60° at P.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the first step in constructing a regular hexagon of side 4cm?
options: Draw a circle with centre O and radius 4cm
Mark a point A on the circumference
With same radius, mark points B, C, D, E, F on the circumference
questions.explanation: The module: 'i) Draw a circle with centre O and radius 4cm. ii) Make a point A on the circumference. iii) With the same radius, 4cm and compass point at A, mark a point B on the circumference.' (Unit 2, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a locus?
options: The path traced by a point as it moves under certain specified conditions
questions.explanation: The module: 'The locus of a point is the path traced by the point as it moves under certain specified condition.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the locus of points equidistant from a fixed point?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Circle: is a locus of points which is equidistant from one point.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the locus of points equidistant from two fixed points?
options: The perpendicular bisector of the line segment joining the points
questions.explanation: The module: 'This is the perpendicular bisector of the line segment with given endpoints.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the locus of points equidistant from two intersecting lines?
options: The angle bisector of the angle formed by the lines
questions.explanation: The module: 'If M is such that it is equidistant from the lines PQ and PR, then the locus of M is the bisector of the angle formed by the two lines.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What is a circumscribed circle of a triangle?
options: A circle that passes through all three vertices of the triangle
A circle that touches all three sides of the triangle
questions.explanation: The module: 'Construct a circle to pass through the points A, B and C.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: How do you construct a circumscribed circle of a triangle?
options: Bisect any two sides of the triangle and use the intersection as centre
Bisect any two angles and use the intersection as centre
Draw a circle with any radius
questions.explanation: The module: 'ii) Bisect any two sides of the triangle. iii) Name the point of intersection of the bisectors and use it as the centre to draw a circle.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What is an inscribed circle of a triangle?
options: A circle that touches all three sides of the triangle
A circle that passes through all three vertices
A circle that passes through two vertices
questions.explanation: The module: 'Construct a circle which touches the three sides of triangle PQR.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: How do you construct an inscribed circle of a triangle?
options: Bisect any two angles of the triangle and use the intersection as centre
Bisect any two sides and use the intersection as centre
Draw a circle with any radius
questions.explanation: The module: 'ii) Bisect any two angles. iii) Name the point of intersection of the bisectors O. iv) Using O as the centre draw a circle inside the triangle such that it touches all the three sides of the triangle.' (Unit 2, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the first step in constructing a perpendicular bisector of a line segment?
options: Draw the line segment and with compass point at one end draw arcs above and below
With compass point at the other end draw arcs
questions.explanation: The module: 'i) Draw a straight line AB of any convenient length. ii) With the compass point at A and with any reasonable radius draw an arc above and below the line AB.' (Unit 2, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the purpose of constructing a perpendicular from a point on a line?
options: To create a 90° angle at that point
questions.explanation: The module describes construction of perpendicular at a point to create a right angle. (Unit 2, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the purpose of constructing a perpendicular from a point outside a line?
options: To find the shortest distance from the point to the line
questions.explanation: The module describes construction of perpendicular from a point outside the line, which gives the shortest distance. (Unit 2, Session 1)

questions.question: In the construction of a perpendicular from a point outside a line, what is the first step after drawing the line and point?
options: With the point as centre, draw arcs to cut the line at two points
Draw a line from the point to the line
questions.explanation: The module: 'ii) With M as the centre, draw arcs to cut the line AB at A and B.' (Unit 2, Session 1)

questions.question: How many lines of symmetry does an equilateral triangle have?
questions.explanation: The module: 'It has three lines of symmetry.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: How many lines of symmetry does an isosceles triangle have?
questions.explanation: The module: 'One-line symmetry which bisects the base and the angles opposite it.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: How many lines of symmetry does a scalene triangle have?
questions.explanation: The module: 'No line symmetry' for scalene triangle. (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the order of rotational symmetry of an equilateral triangle?
questions.explanation: The module: 'It has rotational symmetry of order 3.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the order of rotational symmetry of an isosceles triangle?
questions.explanation: The module: 'It has no rotational symmetry.' (Unit 1, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a rectangle?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Rotational symmetry of order 2' for rectangle. (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a square?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Rotational symmetry order 4' for square. (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a rhombus?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Has rotational symmetry of order 2' for rhombus. (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a parallelogram?
questions.explanation: The module: 'has rotational symmetry of order two' for parallelogram. (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a kite?
questions.explanation: The module: 'No rotational symmetry' for kite. (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a trapezium?
questions.explanation: The module: 'No rotational symmetry' for trapezium. (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: How many lines of symmetry does a rhombus have?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Has two lines of symmetry' for rhombus. (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: How many lines of symmetry does a kite have?
questions.explanation: The module: 'One diagonal as line of symmetry' for kite. (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the difference between a rhombus and a square?
options: A square has all angles 90°; a rhombus does not necessarily
A rhombus has all sides equal; a square does not
A square has no rotational symmetry
questions.explanation: The module defines square as having all angles 90°, rhombus as having all sides equal but angles not necessarily 90°. (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the difference between a rectangle and a parallelogram?
options: A rectangle has all angles 90°; a parallelogram does not necessarily
A rectangle has opposite sides equal; a parallelogram does not
A parallelogram has all sides equal
questions.explanation: The module defines rectangle as having each angle 90°, parallelogram as opposite angles equal but not necessarily 90°. (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the difference between a trapezium and a parallelogram?
options: A trapezium has one pair of parallel sides; a parallelogram has two pairs
A trapezium has two pairs of parallel sides
A trapezium has all sides equal
questions.explanation: The module defines trapezium as having one pair of parallel sides, parallelogram as having two pairs. (Unit 1, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the purpose of a geometry net?
options: To show the faces of a solid laid out flat
questions.explanation: The module: 'When the surface of a three-dimensional figure is laid out flat showing each face of the figure, the pattern obtained is the net.' (Unit 1, Session 5)

questions.question: How many possible nets are there for a cube?
questions.explanation: The module: 'There are altogether 11 possible nets for a cube.' (Unit 1, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the relationship between congruent triangles and similar triangles?
options: All congruent triangles are similar, but not all similar triangles are congruent
questions.explanation: The module: 'If two objects are congruent, then they are also similar.' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: What is the SSS congruence criterion?
options: All three pairs of corresponding sides are equal
Two pairs of sides and the included angle are equal
Two pairs of angles and the included side are equal
The hypotenuse and one side are equal
questions.explanation: The module: 'All three pairs of corresponding sides are equal.' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: What is the SAS congruence criterion?
options: Two pairs of corresponding sides and the corresponding angles between them are equal
Two angles and the included side are equal
Hypotenuse and one side are equal
questions.explanation: The module: 'Two pairs of corresponding sides and the corresponding angles between them are equal.' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: What is the ASA congruence criterion?
options: Two pairs of corresponding angles and the corresponding sides between them are equal
Two sides and the included angle are equal
Hypotenuse and one side are equal
questions.explanation: The module: 'Two pairs of corresponding angles and the corresponding sides between them are equal.' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: What is the RHS congruence criterion?
options: The pair of hypotenuses and another pair of corresponding sides are equal in two right triangles
Two angles and the included side are equal
Two sides and the included angle are equal
questions.explanation: The module: 'The pair of hypotenuses and another pair of corresponding sides are equal in two right triangles.' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: What is the property of similar triangles regarding corresponding sides?
options: Their corresponding sides are in the same ratio
questions.explanation: The module: 'Similar triangles have their corresponding sides in the same ratio.' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: What is the property of similar triangles regarding corresponding angles?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Similar triangles ... have 3 pairs of corresponding angles being equal.' (Unit 1, Session 6)

questions.question: What is a right-angled triangle?
options: A triangle with one of its interior angles being equal to 90°
A triangle with all angles acute
A triangle with all sides equal
A triangle with one obtuse angle
questions.explanation: The module: 'A right angle triangle is a triangle with one of its interior angles being equal to 90°.' (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the hypotenuse of a right-angled triangle?
options: The longest side, always opposite the right angle
The side adjacent to the right angle
The side opposite to a given acute angle
questions.explanation: The module: 'The longest side of a right-angled triangle is called the Hypotenuse. This side is always opposite the right angle.' (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: In a right-angled triangle, the side opposite to a given acute angle is called:
questions.explanation: The module: 'The side opposite to the angle marked A is called Opposite (Opp).' (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: In a right-angled triangle, the side adjacent to a given acute angle is called:
questions.explanation: The module: 'The third side is adjacent to the indicated angle A.' (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: What does Pythagoras' theorem state?
options: In a right-angled triangle, the square of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides
The sum of the angles in a triangle is 180°
The area of a triangle is half the base times height
The perimeter of a triangle is the sum of its sides
questions.explanation: The module: 'Pythagoras theorem states that, in a right angled triangle the square of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides.' (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: If in a right-angled triangle, a = 3, b = 4, what is the length of the hypotenuse c?
questions.explanation: The module gives the example: 3² + 4² = 5², so hypotenuse = 5. (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a Pythagorean triple?
options: A set of three whole numbers that satisfy Pythagoras' theorem
A triangle with three equal sides
A triangle with one right angle
A triangle with all angles acute
questions.explanation: The module: 'Sometimes, the three lengths of a right-angled triangle are all whole numbers. When this occurs, they are called Pythagorean triple or triad. The most commonly known of these is 3, 4, 5.' (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: What are the three common trigonometric ratios?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Three common trigonometric ratios are the sine (sin), cosine (cos), and tangent (tan).' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What does SOH-CAH-TOA help you remember?
options: The definitions of sine, cosine, and tangent
The sum of angles in a triangle
questions.explanation: The module: 'The word soh, cah, toa helps us remember the definitions of sine, cosine, and tangent.' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: According to SOH, sine is:
questions.explanation: The module: 'SOH: Sine is Opposite over Hypotenuse.' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: According to CAH, cosine is:
questions.explanation: The module: 'CAH: Cosine is Adjacent over Hypotenuse.' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: According to TOA, tangent is:
questions.explanation: The module: 'TOA: Tangent is Opposite over Adjacent.' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What are inverse trigonometric functions used for?
options: To find angles when the side ratios are known
To find side ratios when angles are known
To find the area of a triangle
questions.explanation: The module: 'We need inverse trig functions! ... they take side ratios and give angles.' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the angle of elevation?
options: The angle between the horizontal line of sight and the object when looking up
The angle between the horizontal line of sight and the object when looking down
The angle between two horizontal lines
The angle between two vertical lines
questions.explanation: The module: 'If a person stands and looks up at an object, the angle of elevation is the angle between the horizontal line of sight and the object.' (Unit 3, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the angle of depression?
options: The angle between the horizontal line of sight and the object when looking down
The angle between the horizontal line of sight and the object when looking up
The angle between two horizontal lines
The angle between two vertical lines
questions.explanation: The module: 'If a person stands and looks down at an object, the angle of depression is the angle between the horizontal line of sight and the object.' (Unit 3, Session 3)

questions.question: If the angle of elevation of the sun is 60° and a pole casts a shadow of 10 m, what is the height of the pole? (Use tan 60° = √3 ≈ 1.732)
questions.explanation: The module example: Height = 10 × tan 60° = 10 × 1.732 = 17.32 m. (Unit 3, Session 3)

questions.question: What is a vector quantity?
options: A quantity that has both magnitude and direction
A quantity that has only magnitude
A quantity that has only direction
A quantity that has neither magnitude nor direction
questions.explanation: The module: 'A vector quantity is any quantity which has both magnitude and direction.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a scalar quantity?
options: A quantity that has magnitude only
A quantity that has both magnitude and direction
A quantity that has only direction
questions.explanation: The module: 'A scalar quantity has magnitude only. Examples are time, speed, distance, mass, area, etc.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: How are vector quantities represented in diagrams?
options: By arrowed lines; length represents magnitude, arrow shows direction
questions.explanation: The module: 'Vector quantities are represented by arrowed lines in diagrams. The length of the line represents the magnitude and the arrow shows the direction.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the zero vector?
options: A vector with zero components, having zero magnitude
questions.explanation: The module: 'The zero vector is a vector with zero components i.e. (0,0). It has zero magnitude.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the negative of a vector?
options: A vector with the same magnitude but opposite direction
A vector with the same direction
questions.explanation: The module: 'A vector and its inverse have the same magnitude but opposite directions.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: How do you find the magnitude (length) of a vector (x, y)?
questions.explanation: The module: 'the magnitude of AB denoted by |AB| = √(x² + y²).' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: Two vectors are equal if:
options: They have the same magnitude and direction
They have the same magnitude only
They have the same direction only
questions.explanation: The module: 'Two vectors are said to be equal or equivalent if they have the same magnitude and direction. ... In terms of components, two vectors are said to be equal if their corresponding components are the same.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: How do you add two vectors (x1, y1) and (x2, y2)?
questions.explanation: The module: 'To add the vectors (x1, y1) and (x2, y2), we add the corresponding components from each vector: (x1 + x2, y1 + y2).' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: How do you subtract two vectors (x1, y1) and (x2, y2)?
questions.explanation: The module: 'To subtract ... we subtract the corresponding components: (x1 - x2, y1 - y2).' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the resultant of two vectors?
options: The vector that starts from the starting point of the first and ends at the end point of the second when placed end-to-end
questions.explanation: The module: 'When two vectors are placed end to end... then the vector that starts from the starting point of the first to the end point of the second is the resultant.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: How do you multiply a vector by a scalar k?
questions.explanation: The module: 'To find the scalar multiple of a vector, multiply each component of the vector by the scalar.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: When is a scalar multiple positive?
options: When the scalar is positive, the vectors are parallel and in the same direction
When the scalar is positive, they are in opposite directions
questions.explanation: The module: 'When k is positive, it implies the vectors are parallel and in the same direction.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: When is a scalar multiple negative?
options: When the scalar is negative, the vectors are parallel but in opposite directions
When the scalar is negative, they are in the same direction
questions.explanation: The module: 'When k is negative, it implies the vectors are parallel but in opposite directions.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: Two non-zero vectors are parallel if:
options: One is a scalar multiple of the other
questions.explanation: The module: 'Two non-zero vectors are said to be parallel if one is a scalar multiple of the other.' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: How do you find a vector perpendicular to a given vector (a, b)?
options: Rotate the vector through 90° or 270°, e.g., (-b, a) or (b, -a)
questions.explanation: The module: 'The perpendicular to a vector is found by rotating the vector through an angle of 90° or 270°. For example, the vector (2, 1) is perpendicular to the vectors (-1, 2) and (1, -2).' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: What is a position vector?
options: A vector that represents the position of a point relative to an origin
A vector that has magnitude only
A vector that has direction only
questions.explanation: The module: 'Position vectors are denoted by small letters printed in bold type... If the point A is referenced to O then OA is the position vector of A relative to O.' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: How do you find the vector joining two points A and B?
options: Subtract the position vector of A from the position vector of B: AB = b - a
questions.explanation: The module: 'AB = b - a' (Unit 4, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the midpoint theorem for vectors?
options: The position vector of the midpoint of AB is half the sum of the position vectors of A and B
The midpoint is the average of the coordinates
The midpoint is the sum of the vectors
The midpoint is the difference of the vectors
questions.explanation: The module: 'Thus the position vector of P is half the sum of the position vectors of A and B. This is called the midpoint theorem.' (Unit 4, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a bearing?
options: An angle measured clockwise from the north direction
An angle measured anticlockwise from the south
An angle measured from the east
An angle measured from the west
questions.explanation: The module: 'A bearing is an angle, measured clockwise from the north direction.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: Bearings are usually given in how many digits?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Bearings are usually given in three digits.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: What is a back bearing?
options: The bearing of the first point from the second point
The bearing of the second point from the first point
questions.explanation: The module: 'The bearing of A from B is referred to as the back bearing of B from A.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: How do you convert a vector from magnitude-bearing form to component form?
options: Use the acute angle with the x-axis: x-component = magnitude × cos θ, y-component = magnitude × sin θ
Add the magnitude to the bearing
questions.explanation: The module: 'If the magnitude and bearing of any vector are given, its components are easily found. ... the x-component of the vector is v cos θ and the y-component is v sin θ.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the process of expressing a vector into its components called?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The process of expressing a vector into its components is known as resolution of a vector.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: In a right-angled triangle, if the opposite side is 3 and the adjacent is 4, what is tan θ?
questions.explanation: Tangent = Opposite/Adjacent = 3/4. (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the sine of an angle in a right-angled triangle?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Sine is Opposite over Hypotenuse.' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the cosine of an angle in a right-angled triangle?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Cosine is Adjacent over Hypotenuse.' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the tangent of an angle in a right-angled triangle?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Tangent is Opposite over Adjacent.' (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: If sin θ = 3/5, what is the value of θ (to the nearest degree)?
questions.explanation: Using inverse sine: sin⁻¹(3/5) ≈ 36.87° ≈ 37°. (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: In a right-angled triangle, if the hypotenuse is 10 and the angle is 30°, what is the length of the opposite side? (sin 30° = 0.5)
questions.explanation: Opposite = hypotenuse × sin 30° = 10 × 0.5 = 5. (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: In a right-angled triangle, if the adjacent side is 8 and the angle is 60°, what is the length of the hypotenuse? (cos 60° = 0.5)
questions.explanation: Hypotenuse = adjacent / cos 60° = 8 / 0.5 = 16. (Unit 3, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the angle of elevation of the sun if a pole of height 10 m casts a shadow of 10 m?
questions.explanation: tan θ = opposite/adjacent = 10/10 = 1, so θ = 45°. (Unit 3, Session 3)

questions.question: A ladder 5 m long leans against a wall. If the foot of the ladder is 3 m from the wall, how high up the wall does it reach?
questions.explanation: Using Pythagoras: height² = 5² - 3² = 25 - 9 = 16, height = 4 m. (Unit 3, Session 1)

questions.question: If a vector has components (3, 4), what is its magnitude?
questions.explanation: Magnitude = √(3² + 4²) = √(9+16) = √25 = 5. (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: If a = (2, 3) and b = (4, 1), what is a + b?
If a = (5, 2) and b = (3, 1), what is a - b?
If a = (2, 3), what is 3a?
If two vectors are parallel, what is true about them?
options: One is a scalar multiple of the other
questions.explanation: The module: 'Two non-zero vectors are said to be parallel if one is a scalar multiple of the other.' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the bearing of B from A if the back bearing is 205°?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The bearing of A from B is 205 degrees. The bearing of B from A is 025 degrees.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the formula for the area of a sector?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Area of sector = (θ/360) × πr².' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the formula for the arc length of a sector?
questions.explanation: The module: 'arc length AB = (θ/360) × 2πr.' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the circumference of a circle of radius r?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The circumference of a circle, C with radius, r is given by C = 2πr.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the area of a circle of radius r?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The area of a circle, A with radius, r is given by A = πr².' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the volume of a cylinder with radius r and height h?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Volume of a cylinder is given by V = πr²h.' (Unit 5, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the curved surface area of a cylinder?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Curved surface area of a cylinder is given by the formula: A = 2πrh.' (Unit 5, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the total surface area of a closed cylinder?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Total Surface area of a closed (solid) cylinder ... A = 2πr² + 2πrh.' (Unit 5, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the volume of a cone?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Volume of a cone, V = (1/3)πr²h.' (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the curved surface area of a cone?
options: A = πrl, where l is slant height
questions.explanation: The module: 'Curved surface area of a cone = πrl.' (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the total surface area of a cone?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Total surface area of a cone = πr² + πrl.' (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the volume of a rectangular pyramid?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Volume of a rectangular pyramid is given by V = (1/3)Ah.' (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the volume of a sphere?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Volume of a sphere is given by V = (4/3)πr³.' (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the surface area of a sphere?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The surface area of a sphere is given by A = 4πr².' (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a vector quantity?
options: A quantity that has both magnitude and direction
A quantity that has only magnitude
A quantity that has only direction
questions.explanation: The module: 'A vector quantity is any quantity which has both magnitude and direction.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a scalar quantity?
options: A quantity that has magnitude only
A quantity that has both magnitude and direction
A quantity that has only direction
questions.explanation: The module: 'A scalar quantity has magnitude only. Examples are time, speed, distance, mass, area, etc.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: How are vector quantities represented in diagrams?
options: By arrowed lines; length represents magnitude, arrow shows direction
questions.explanation: The module: 'Vector quantities are represented by arrowed lines in diagrams. The length of the line represents the magnitude and the arrow shows the direction.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the zero vector?
options: A vector with zero components, having zero magnitude
questions.explanation: The module: 'The zero vector is a vector with zero components i.e. (0,0). It has zero magnitude.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the negative of a vector?
options: A vector with the same magnitude but opposite direction
A vector with the same direction
questions.explanation: The module: 'A vector and its inverse have the same magnitude but opposite directions.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: How do you find the magnitude (length) of a vector (x, y)?
questions.explanation: The module: 'the magnitude of AB denoted by |AB| = √(x² + y²).' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: Two vectors are equal if:
options: They have the same magnitude and direction
They have the same magnitude only
They have the same direction only
questions.explanation: The module: 'Two vectors are said to be equal or equivalent if they have the same magnitude and direction. ... In terms of components, two vectors are said to be equal if their corresponding components are the same.' (Unit 4, Session 1)

questions.question: How do you add two vectors (x1, y1) and (x2, y2)?
questions.explanation: The module: 'To add the vectors (x1, y1) and (x2, y2), we add the corresponding components from each vector: (x1 + x2, y1 + y2).' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: How do you subtract two vectors (x1, y1) and (x2, y2)?
questions.explanation: The module: 'To subtract ... we subtract the corresponding components: (x1 - x2, y1 - y2).' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the resultant of two vectors?
options: The vector that starts from the starting point of the first and ends at the end point of the second when placed end-to-end
questions.explanation: The module: 'When two vectors are placed end to end... then the vector that starts from the starting point of the first to the end point of the second is the resultant.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: How do you multiply a vector by a scalar k?
questions.explanation: The module: 'To find the scalar multiple of a vector, multiply each component of the vector by the scalar.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: When is a scalar multiple positive?
options: When the scalar is positive, the vectors are parallel and in the same direction
When the scalar is positive, they are in opposite directions
questions.explanation: The module: 'When k is positive, it implies the vectors are parallel and in the same direction.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: When is a scalar multiple negative?
options: When the scalar is negative, the vectors are parallel but in opposite directions
When the scalar is negative, they are in the same direction
questions.explanation: The module: 'When k is negative, it implies the vectors are parallel but in opposite directions.' (Unit 4, Session 2)

questions.question: Two non-zero vectors are parallel if:
options: One is a scalar multiple of the other
questions.explanation: The module: 'Two non-zero vectors are said to be parallel if one is a scalar multiple of the other.' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: How do you find a vector perpendicular to a given vector (a, b)?
options: Rotate the vector through 90° or 270°, e.g., (-b, a) or (b, -a)
questions.explanation: The module: 'The perpendicular to a vector is found by rotating the vector through an angle of 90° or 270°. For example, the vector (2, 1) is perpendicular to the vectors (-1, 2) and (1, -2).' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: What is a position vector?
options: A vector that represents the position of a point relative to an origin
A vector that has magnitude only
A vector that has direction only
questions.explanation: The module: 'Position vectors are denoted by small letters printed in bold type... If the point A is referenced to O then OA is the position vector of A relative to O.' (Unit 4, Session 3)

questions.question: How do you find the vector joining two points A and B?
options: Subtract the position vector of A from the position vector of B: AB = b - a
questions.explanation: The module: 'AB = b - a' (Unit 4, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the midpoint theorem for vectors?
options: The position vector of the midpoint of AB is half the sum of the position vectors of A and B
The midpoint is the average of the coordinates
The midpoint is the sum of the vectors
The midpoint is the difference of the vectors
questions.explanation: The module: 'Thus the position vector of P is half the sum of the position vectors of A and B. This is called the midpoint theorem.' (Unit 4, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a bearing?
options: An angle measured clockwise from the north direction
An angle measured anticlockwise from the south
An angle measured from the east
An angle measured from the west
questions.explanation: The module: 'A bearing is an angle, measured clockwise from the north direction.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: Bearings are usually given in how many digits?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Bearings are usually given in three digits.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: What is a back bearing?
options: The bearing of the first point from the second point
The bearing of the second point from the first point
questions.explanation: The module: 'The bearing of A from B is referred to as the back bearing of B from A.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: How do you convert a vector from magnitude-bearing form to component form?
options: Use the acute angle with the x-axis: x-component = magnitude × cos θ, y-component = magnitude × sin θ
Add the magnitude to the bearing
questions.explanation: The module: 'If the magnitude and bearing of any vector are given, its components are easily found. ... the x-component of the vector is v cos θ and the y-component is v sin θ.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the process of expressing a vector into its components called?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The process of expressing a vector into its components is known as resolution of a vector.' (Unit 4, Session 5)

questions.question: What is a circle?
options: A plane figure bounded by a curved line such that all points are at the same distance from a fixed point called the centre
questions.explanation: The module: 'A circle is a plane figure bounded by a curved line such that the set of all points in the plane are at the same distance from a fixed point called the centre.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the circumference of a circle?
options: The total distance around the circle, i.e., its perimeter
The distance across the circle through the centre
The distance from the centre to the edge
questions.explanation: The module: 'Circumference: It is the total distance around the circle. In other words, it is the perimeter of the circle.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the radius of a circle?
options: A line drawn from the centre to any point on the circumference
A line passing through the centre and touching two points on the circumference
The total distance around the circle
questions.explanation: The module: 'Radius: It is a line drawn from the centre of a circle to any point on the circumference of the circle.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the diameter of a circle?
options: A line which divides the circle into two equal parts; it is twice the radius
A line from the centre to the edge
The total distance around the circle
questions.explanation: The module: 'Diameter: It is the line which divides the circle into two equal parts. It is twice the radius of the circle.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a chord of a circle?
options: A straight line joining any two points on the circumference
A line from the centre to the circumference
A line that touches the circle at one point
The total distance around the circle
questions.explanation: The module: 'Chord: It is a straight line joining any two points on the circumference of a circle.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is an arc of a circle?
options: Any portion or part of the circumference
A straight line joining two points on the circumference
The area bounded by a chord and an arc
A line from the centre to the circumference
questions.explanation: The module: 'Arc: It is any portion or part of the circumference of the circle.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a segment of a circle?
options: The area bounded by a chord and the arc it cuts off
The area bounded by two radii and an arc
A line that touches the circle at one point
questions.explanation: The module: 'Segment: This is the area bounded by a chord and the arc it cuts off.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a sector of a circle?
options: An area bounded by two radii and the arc cut off in between them
The area bounded by a chord and an arc
A line that touches the circle at one point
questions.explanation: The module: 'A sector is an area bounded by two radii and the arc cut off in between them.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a semi-circle?
options: The area bounded by a chord and an arc
A line that touches the circle at one point
questions.explanation: The module: 'Semi-circle: It is half of a circle.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a quadrant of a circle?
options: A quarter of the total area of a circle
The area bounded by a chord and an arc
A line that touches the circle at one point
questions.explanation: The module: 'Quadrant: It is a quarter of the total area of a circle.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a tangent to a circle?
options: A straight line which touches a circle at only one point and does not cut the circumference when extended
A line that cuts the circle at two points
A line from the centre to the circumference
questions.explanation: The module: 'Tangent: Any straight line which touches a circle at only one point and does not cut the circumference or arc when extended is called a tangent.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What are concentric circles?
options: Two circles with the same centre but different radii
Two circles with the same radius
questions.explanation: The module: 'Concentric Circles: Two circles with the same centre but different radii are said to be concentric.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the formula for the circumference of a circle with radius r?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The circumference of a circle, C with radius, r is given by C = 2πr.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the formula for the area of a circle with radius r?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The area of a circle, A with radius, r is given by A = πr².' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the circumference of a semi-circle with radius r?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Half Circumference = (1/2) × circumference of a circle = (1/2) × 2πr = πr.' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the area of a semi-circle with radius r?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Area of a semi-circle = (1/2) × area of a circle = (1/2)πr².' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the area of a quadrant of a circle with radius r?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Area of a quadrant = (1/4) × area of a circle = (1/4)πr².' (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the formula for the length of an arc subtending an angle θ at the centre of a circle of radius r?
questions.explanation: The module: 'arc length AB = (θ/360) × 2πr.' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the formula for the area of a sector subtending an angle θ at the centre of a circle of radius r?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Area of sector = (θ/360) × πr².' (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the volume of a cylinder with radius r and height h?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Volume of a cylinder is given by V = πr²h.' (Unit 5, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the curved surface area of a cylinder with radius r and height h?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Curved surface area of a cylinder is given by the formula: A = 2πrh.' (Unit 5, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the total surface area of a closed cylinder with radius r and height h?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Total surface area of a closed (solid) cylinder ... A = 2πr² + 2πrh.' (Unit 5, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the total surface area of an open cylinder (open at one end)?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Total surface area of an open cylinder: A = πr² + 2πrh.' (Unit 5, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the volume of a triangular prism?
options: V = (1/2) × base length × height of triangle × length of prism
questions.explanation: The module: 'Volume of a triangular prism is given by V = (1/2)bh × H, where b is base of triangle, h is height of triangle, and H is length of prism.' (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the volume of a cone with radius r and height h?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Volume of a cone, V = (1/3)πr²h.' (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the curved surface area of a cone with radius r and slant height l?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Curved surface area of a cone = πrl.' (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the total surface area of a cone with radius r and slant height l?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Total surface area of a cone = πr² + πrl.' (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the volume of a rectangular pyramid?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Volume of a rectangular pyramid is given by V = (1/3)Ah.' (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the volume of a square pyramid?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Volume of a square pyramid: V = (1/3) × area of base × height = (1/3) × side² × height.' (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the volume of a sphere with radius r?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Volume of a sphere is given by V = (4/3)πr³.' (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the surface area of a sphere with radius r?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The surface area of a sphere is given by A = 4πr².' (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the relationship between slant height l, height h, and radius r of a cone?
questions.explanation: The module: 'l² = r² + h²' for a cone. (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: A cylinder has radius 7 cm and height 10 cm. What is its volume? (Use π = 22/7)
questions.explanation: Volume = πr²h = (22/7) × 7² × 10 = 22 × 7 × 10 = 1540 cm³. (Unit 5, Session 3)

questions.question: A cone has radius 3 cm and height 4 cm. What is its slant height?
questions.explanation: Slant height l = √(r² + h²) = √(3² + 4²) = √(9+16) = √25 = 5 cm. (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the diameter of a circle if its circumference is 44 cm? (Use π = 22/7)
A sector has angle 60° and radius 7 cm. What is its area? (Use π = 22/7)
What is the length of a major arc if the minor arc is 10 cm and the total circumference is 40 cm?
questions.explanation: Major arc = total circumference - minor arc = 40 - 10 = 30 cm. (Unit 5, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the volume of a sphere with radius 7 cm? (Use π = 22/7)
What is the total surface area of a cylinder with radius 3 cm and height 5 cm? (Use π = 22/7)
questions.explanation: Total surface area = 2πr² + 2πrh = 2πr(r+h) = 2 × (22/7) × 3 × (3+5) = 2 × (22/7) × 3 × 8 = 150.86 cm². (Unit 5, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the volume of a cone with radius 5 cm and height 12 cm? (Use π = 22/7)
What is the area of a circle with diameter 14 cm? (Use π = 22/7)
questions.explanation: Radius = 7 cm, area = πr² = (22/7) × 7² = 154 cm². (Unit 5, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the curved surface area of a cylinder with radius 7 cm and height 10 cm? (Use π = 22/7)
questions.explanation: Curved surface area = 2πrh = 2 × (22/7) × 7 × 10 = 440 cm². (Unit 5, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the volume of a rectangular pyramid with base length 6 cm, width 4 cm, and height 9 cm?
questions.explanation: Volume = (1/3) × base area × height = (1/3) × (6 × 4) × 9 = 72 cm³. (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the surface area of a sphere with radius 3 cm? (Use π = 22/7)
questions.explanation: Surface area = 4πr² = 4 × (22/7) × 3² = 4 × (22/7) × 9 = 113.14 cm². (Unit 5, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the slant height of a cone if its radius is 4 cm and height is 3 cm?
A cylinder has volume 440 cm³ and height 10 cm. What is its radius? (Use π = 22/7)
What is the length of an arc subtending an angle of 90° at the centre of a circle with radius 7 cm? (Use π = 22/7)
What is the area of a sector with angle 45° and radius 14 cm? (Use π = 22/7)
What are latitudes?
options: Imaginary lines that run around the earth, perpendicular to the axis of the earth
Imaginary lines that run from north to south
Imaginary lines that divide the earth into two hemispheres
questions.explanation: The module: 'Latitudes are imaginary lines that run around the earth and their planes are perpendicular to the axis of the earth.' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the equator?
options: The latitude that divides the earth into two equal parts; it is 0°
The longitude that passes through Greenwich
The line that runs from north to south
questions.explanation: The module: 'The equator is the latitude that divides the earth into two equal parts. It is 0°.' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What are longitudes/meridians?
options: Circles passing through the north and south poles
Lines that are parallel to the equator
Lines that divide the earth into northern and southern hemispheres
questions.explanation: The module: 'Longitudes/meridians: They are circles passing through the north and south poles.' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the prime meridian?
options: The main meridian of longitude, 0°, also called the Greenwich meridian
The meridian that passes through the Pacific Ocean
questions.explanation: The module: 'The main meridian of longitude is the prime meridian, 0°. It is also called the Greenwich meridian since it runs through the Royal Observatory at Greenwich.' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the range of latitudes?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Parallels of latitude range from 90°N (North Pole) to 90°S (South Pole).' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the range of longitudes?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Meridians of longitude range from 180°E to 180°W.' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a great circle?
options: A circle that divides the sphere into two equal parts, with radius equal to the sphere's radius
A circle that does not pass through the centre
A circle on the equator only
questions.explanation: The module: 'A slice through the centre of a sphere is called a great circle, and its radius is the same as that of the sphere. Hence great circles divide the sphere into two equal parts.' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a small circle?
options: A circle on a sphere whose radius is smaller than that of a great circle
A circle that divides the sphere into two equal parts
A circle with the same radius as the sphere
A circle that passes through the poles
questions.explanation: The module: 'Any other slice is called a small circle, because its radius is smaller than that of a great circle.' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: How do you find the distance between two places on the same meridian?
options: Use the arc length formula along the great circle: distance = (θ/360) × 2πR, where θ is the difference in latitude
questions.explanation: The module: 'The meridian is a great circle, so distance = (θ/360) × 2πR.' (Unit 6, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the distance between two places on the equator?
options: Use the great circle distance formula with the difference in longitude
questions.explanation: The module: 'The Equator is a great circle, and hence finding the distance between two points on the Equator uses the same calculation process as for finding the distance between two points on the same meridian of longitude.' (Unit 6, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the distance along a parallel of latitude?
options: Use the small circle formula: distance = (α/360) × 2πR cos θ, where θ is the latitude and α is the difference in longitude
questions.explanation: The module: 'distance along a small circle (circle of latitude) = (α/360) × 2πR cos θ.' (Unit 6, Session 3)

questions.question: How do you find the shortest distance between two points on the earth's surface?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The shortest distance between two points on the earth’s surface is that along a great circle.' (Unit 6, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the relationship between longitude and time?
options: The earth rotates 360° in 24 hours, so 1° corresponds to 4 minutes, and 15° corresponds to 1 hour
questions.explanation: The module: 'The earth rotates through 360° about its axis every 24 hours... Therefore, for every 1° change in longitude there is a corresponding change in time of 4 minutes, or there is a difference of 1 hour between two meridians 15° apart.' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What is Greenwich Mean Time (GMT)?
options: The time at the International Date Line
questions.explanation: The module: 'Local time at Greenwich is called Greenwich Mean Time, GMT.' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: If it is 12:00 noon at Greenwich (0°), what is the local time at 15°E?
questions.explanation: 15°E is ahead of GMT by 1 hour (15° = 1 hour). So 12:00 + 1 hour = 1:00 PM. (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: If it is 12:00 noon at Greenwich, what is the local time at 15°W?
questions.explanation: 15°W is behind GMT by 1 hour, so 12:00 - 1 hour = 11:00 AM. (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the time difference between two places with longitude difference of 30°?
questions.explanation: 30° × 4 minutes/° = 120 minutes = 2 hours. (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the formula for average speed?
options: Average speed = Total distance travelled / Total time taken
Average speed = Total time / Total distance
questions.explanation: The module: 'Average speed = Total distance travelled / Total time taken.' (Unit 6, Session 4)

questions.question: How do you calculate the total journey time when there is a time difference due to longitude?
options: Add or subtract the time difference from the difference in local times
questions.explanation: The module: 'Total journey time = (local arrival time - local departure time) ± time difference due to longitude.' (Unit 6, Session 4)

questions.question: If a flight leaves at 10:00 AM local time and arrives at 2:00 PM local time, and the time difference due to longitude is +1 hour, what is the total journey time?
questions.explanation: Journey time = (14:00 - 10:00) - 1 hour (since arrival local is ahead) = 4 - 1 = 3 hours. (Unit 6, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the distance between two points on the same latitude if the latitude is 60°N and the difference in longitude is 30°? (Take R = 6370 km, cos 60° = 0.5)
What is the shortest distance between two points on latitude 60°N with longitude difference 30°? (Use great circle formula)
questions.explanation: The shortest distance is along the great circle, which is less than the small circle distance. It would be calculated using spherical trigonometry, but the module gives an example showing the great circle distance is shorter. (Unit 6, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the latitude of the North Pole?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Parallels of latitude range from 90°N (North Pole) to 90°S (South Pole).' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the longitude of the International Date Line?
