questions.explanation: The module: '180°E and 180°W are actually the same meridian, on the opposite side of the Earth to the prime meridian.' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: If two places are on different sides of the equator, how do you find the difference in latitude?
questions.explanation: The module: 'If P is θ north of the equator and Q is α south of the equator, then the difference in latitude between them is given by (θ + α).' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: If two places are on the same side of the equator, how do you find the difference in latitude?
questions.explanation: The module: 'If P and Q are on the same side of the equator, then the difference in latitude is (θ - α).' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the local time at a place 37°E when it is 12:00 noon at Greenwich? (4 minutes per degree)
questions.explanation: 37° × 4 min = 148 min = 2 hr 28 min ahead. So 12:00 + 2:28 = 2:28 PM. (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the distance between two points on the same meridian if their latitudes are 20°N and 30°N? (Take R = 6370 km)
questions.explanation: Difference in latitude = 10°, distance = (10/360) × 2πR = (1/36) × 2π × 6370 ≈ 1110 km. (Unit 6, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the distance between two points on the same meridian if their latitudes are 20°N and 30°S? (Take R = 6370 km)
questions.explanation: Difference in latitude = 20° + 30° = 50°, distance = (50/360) × 2πR ≈ 5550 km. (Unit 6, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the angle of latitude?
options: The angle the latitude makes with the Equator at the centre of the Earth
The angle between the equator and the poles
questions.explanation: The module: 'The angle of latitude is the angle the latitude makes with the Equator at the centre, O, of the Earth.' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the angle of longitude?
options: The angle the meridian makes with the prime meridian at the centre of the Earth
questions.explanation: The module: 'The angle of longitude is the angle the meridian makes with the prime meridian at the centre, O, of the Earth.' (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: If the local time at 0° is 12:00 noon, what is the local time at 180°E?
questions.explanation: 180° = 12 hours ahead, so 12:00 noon + 12 hours = 12:00 midnight (next day). (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: If the local time at 180°E is 12:00 midnight on Monday, what is the local time at 0°?
questions.explanation: 180°E is 12 hours ahead of 0°, so at 0° it is 12 hours behind: 12:00 midnight Monday - 12 hours = 12:00 noon Sunday. (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the time difference between 45°E and 45°W?
questions.explanation: 45°E is +3 hours from GMT, 45°W is -3 hours, so difference = 6 hours. (Unit 6, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the great circle distance between two points on the same latitude? (general concept)
options: It is shorter than the distance along the parallel
It is longer than the distance along the parallel
questions.explanation: The module shows that the great circle distance is shorter than the distance along the parallel of latitude. (Unit 6, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the angular distance between two points on the same meridian if their latitudes are 10°N and 20°N?
questions.explanation: Difference in latitude = 20° - 10° = 10°. (Unit 6, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the angular distance between two points on the same meridian if their latitudes are 10°N and 10°S?
questions.explanation: Add the latitudes: 10° + 10° = 20°. (Unit 6, Session 2)

questions.question: How is the distance along a parallel of latitude calculated?
options: Using the small circle formula: distance = (angle difference / 360) × 2πR cos(latitude)
questions.explanation: The module: 'The length of the circle parallel of latitude θ is 2πR cos θ.' (Unit 6, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the circumference of the parallel of latitude 60°N? (R = 6370 km)
questions.explanation: The module: 'The length of the circle parallel of latitude θ is 2πR cos θ.' So at 60°, cos 60° = 0.5, circumference = 2πR × 0.5 = πR ≈ 20000 km. (Unit 6, Session 3)

questions.question: What is statistics?
options: A branch of science which deals with the collection, classification, analysis and interpretation of data to make informed decisions
A branch of mathematics that deals with numbers only
questions.explanation: The module: 'Statistics is a branch of science which deals with the collection, classification, analyse and interpretation of data and to make inform decisions.' (Unit 7, Session 1)

questions.question: What are the two broad areas of statistics?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The two broad areas of this science are descriptive statistics and inferential statistics.' (Unit 7, Session 1)

questions.question: What is descriptive statistics?
options: It consists of collection, organization, summarization, and presentation of data
It involves making predictions from data
It is the study of probability
It is the study of samples
questions.explanation: The module: 'Descriptive Statistics: It consists of collection, organization, summarization, and presentation of data.' (Unit 7, Session 1)

questions.question: What is inferential statistics?
options: It is used to interpret a given data in order to make predictions
It is the collection of data
It is the presentation of data
It is the study of populations
questions.explanation: The module: 'Inferential Statistics: It is the type of statistics which is used to interpret a given data in order to make predictions.' (Unit 7, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a population in statistics?
options: All observations or measurements of interest, the totality of all subjects possessing certain common traits
questions.explanation: The module: 'The term population refers to all observation or measurement of interest. It is the totality of all subjects possessing certain common trait.' (Unit 7, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a random sample?
options: A sample where every element in the population has the same chance of being selected
A sample of the entire population
questions.explanation: The module: 'If a sample is obtained, such that every element in the population has the same chance of being selected, it is called random sample.' (Unit 7, Session 1)

questions.question: Which method of data collection involves verbal interaction between questioner and respondent?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Interview: With this method, there is a verbal interaction between the questioner and the respondent.' (Unit 7, Session 1)

questions.question: Which method of data collection involves written interaction?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Questionnaire: This involves a written interaction between the questioner and the respondent.' (Unit 7, Session 1)

questions.question: Which method of data collection involves taking a closer look at the population without asking questions?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Observation: This is when the researcher collects data from the population or sample by taking a closer look at the population for the observable characteristics without asking questions.' (Unit 7, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a frequency distribution?
options: A tabular arrangement of data showing the frequency of each value or class
questions.explanation: The module shows frequency distributions as tables with tally and frequency columns. (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is an array?
options: A matrix of rows and columns of numbers arranged in some order, usually ascending
questions.explanation: The module: 'An array is a matrix of rows and columns of numbers which have been arranged in some order (preferably ascending).' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the mode of a data set?
options: The value that occurs most often
questions.explanation: The module: 'The mode of a set of data is the value which occurs most often.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the median of a data set?
options: The middle score for a set of data that has been arranged in order of magnitude
The value that occurs most often
questions.explanation: The module: 'The median is the middle score for a set of data that has been arranged in order of magnitude.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the mean of a data set?
options: The sum of all values divided by the number of values
questions.explanation: The module: 'The mean (or average) is the sum of all the values in the data set divided by the number of values in the data set.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a bar chart?
options: A presentation using rectangular bars of equal widths with lengths proportional to the magnitude of each item
A circular graph divided into sectors
questions.explanation: The module: 'Bar charts: In this presentation, we use rectangular bars of equal widths for the items, but with lengths proportional to the magnitude or size of each item.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a pie chart?
options: A circular graph divided into sectors, each representing a different category with angle proportional to frequency
questions.explanation: The module: 'A pie chart is a circular graph divided into sectors; each sector representing a different category. The angle of each sector is proportional to the frequency.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a stem and leaf plot?
options: A diagram used to represent raw data without loss of information, where the leaves are the last digits and the stems are the remaining part
questions.explanation: The module: 'Stem and leaf diagrams, or stem plots, are used to represent raw data, that is, individual observations, without loss of information. The ‘leaves’ are actually the last digits of the values while the ‘stems’ are the remaining part.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a histogram?
options: A graphical display of a frequency distribution using rectangular bars placed side by side
questions.explanation: The module: 'A histogram is a graphical display of a frequency distribution. It consists of rectangular bars placed side by side.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a frequency polygon?
options: A graph formed by joining the midpoints of the tops of the rectangles in a histogram
questions.explanation: The module: 'To draw the frequency polygon, we mark the midpoint of the tops of the rectangles of the histogram and join them by straight line segments.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is cumulative frequency?
options: The total number of observations in all classes up to and including a given class
The frequency of a single class
questions.explanation: The module: 'A cumulative frequency distribution shows, for each class the total number of observations in all classes up to and including that class.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is an ogive?
questions.explanation: The module: 'A graphical method used to present a cumulative frequency is called an Ogive.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a measure of central tendency?
options: A single value that attempts to describe a set of data by identifying the central position
questions.explanation: The module: 'A measure of central tendency is a single value that attempts to describe a set of data by identifying the central position within that set of data.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What are quartiles?
options: Numbers that separate the data into quarters
Numbers that separate the data into tenths
Numbers that separate the data into hundredths
questions.explanation: The module: 'Quartiles are numbers that separate the data into quarters.' (Unit 7, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the interquartile range (IQR)?
options: The difference between the third quartile (Q3) and the first quartile (Q1)
The difference between the maximum and minimum
The average of Q1 and Q3
The sum of Q1 and Q3
questions.explanation: The module: 'The interquartile range is a number that indicates the spread of the middle half or the middle 50% of the data. It is the difference between the third quartile (Q3) and the first quartile (Q1). IQR = Q3 – Q1.' (Unit 7, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the semi-interquartile range?
options: Half the difference between Q3 and Q1
The difference between Q3 and Q1
The average of Q1 and Q3
The sum of Q1 and Q3
questions.explanation: The module: 'The semi-interquartile range is one-half the difference between the first and third quartiles.' (Unit 7, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a percentile?
options: A measure of relative standing; percentiles divide a set of observations into 100 equal parts
questions.explanation: The module: 'Percentiles are measures of relative standing of an observation within a data set. Percentiles divide a set of observations into 100 equal parts.' (Unit 7, Session 3)

questions.question: What is a decile?
options: One of the nine values that divide the sorted data into 10 equal parts
One of the four values that divide data into quarters
One of the hundred values that divide data into hundredths
questions.explanation: The module: 'A decile is any of the nine values that divide the sorted data into 10 equal parts.' (Unit 7, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the range of a data set?
options: The difference between the largest and the smallest value
questions.explanation: The module: 'The difference between the largest and the smallest value of a data, is termed as the range of the distribution.' (Unit 7, Session 4)

questions.question: What is mean deviation?
options: The sum of the absolute values of the deviations from the mean divided by the number of items
The sum of deviations from the mean
The difference between the largest and smallest values
questions.explanation: The module: 'The mean deviation is the sum of the absolute values of the deviations from the mean divided by the number of items.' (Unit 7, Session 4)

questions.question: What is variance?
options: The average of squared deviations from the mean
questions.explanation: The module: 'If we divide this sum [of squared deviations] by the number of observations, we obtain the average of deviations, which is called variance.' (Unit 7, Session 4)

questions.question: What is standard deviation?
options: The positive square root of the variance
questions.explanation: The module: 'If we take the positive square root of the variance, we obtain ... standard deviation.' (Unit 7, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the formula for standard deviation for raw data?
questions.explanation: The module: 'For raw data: σ = √(Σx²/n - (Σx/n)²).' (Unit 7, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the formula for standard deviation for grouped data (discrete)?
questions.explanation: The module: 'For discrete data: σ = √(Σfx²/Σf - (Σfx/Σf)²).' (Unit 7, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a class interval?
options: A group into which data values are put, e.g., 30-39
The difference between the upper and lower class boundaries
questions.explanation: The module: 'The groups into which the values are put are called ‘classes’ eg. 30-39 is a class.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a class boundary?
options: The lower and upper values that mark common points between classes, found by subtracting half the gap between classes from the lower limit and adding to the upper limit
questions.explanation: The module: 'Class Boundaries: These are the lower and the upper values of a class that mark common points between classes. To find the class boundaries, we subtract the upper class limit of the first class from the lower class limit of the second class (i.e. 10-9=1). Then we divide the result by 2. (ie. 0.5). We then subtract 0.5 from all the lower class limits and add 0.5 to all the upper class limits.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a class mark?
options: The midpoint of a class, obtained by adding the lower and upper class limits and dividing by 2
questions.explanation: The module: 'Class Mark: The midpoint of a class is called the ‘class mark’ or the ‘class midpoint’ of that class. It is obtained by adding the lower and upper class limits and dividing the result by 2.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the class width or class size?
options: The difference between the upper and lower class boundaries of a class
The difference between the upper and lower class limits
questions.explanation: The module: 'Class Width or Class Size: This is the difference between the upper and lower class boundaries of a class.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a component bar chart?
options: A bar chart where each total figure is made up of two or more components
questions.explanation: The module: 'Component Bar Chart: This is drawn when each total figure is made up of two or more figures.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is a multiple bar chart?
options: A bar chart used to show the variation of two or more similar categories
questions.explanation: The module: 'Multiple Bar Chart: This can be used to show the variation of two or more similar categories.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the 50th percentile also known as?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The 50th percentile is the median by definition.' (Unit 7, Session 3)

questions.question: What are the 25th and 75th percentiles known as?
options: The lower quartile (Q1) and upper quartile (Q3)
questions.explanation: The module: '25th and 75th percentiles are the lower (Q1) and upper quartiles (Q3) respectively.' (Unit 7, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the formula for the mth percentile for grouped data?
questions.explanation: The module: 'The mth percentile for a grouped data is given by P_m = l + (h/f) × (m/100 × n - c).' (Unit 7, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the formula for the mth decile for grouped data?
questions.explanation: The module: 'Deciles class for D_m can be calculated from (m/10 × n).' The formula is similar to percentiles but with 10 instead of 100. (Unit 7, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the purpose of a cumulative frequency curve?
options: To show the total frequency up to each value or class
To show the frequency of each class
questions.explanation: The module: 'A cumulative frequency distribution shows, for each class the total number of observations in all classes up to and including that class.' (Unit 7, Session 2)

questions.question: What is probability?
options: The study of events which may or may not occur, assigning a numerical value to the likelihood of an event
questions.explanation: The module: 'Probability is the study of events which may or may not occur. The numerical value which is assigned to the likelihood of an event occurring is called is called the probability of the event.' (Unit 8, Session 1)

questions.question: What is an experiment in probability?
options: Any process that generates well-defined outcomes
questions.explanation: The module: 'An experiment: This is any process that generates well-defined outcomes.' (Unit 8, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a trial in probability?
options: The set of all possible outcomes
A subset of the sample space
questions.explanation: The module: 'Trial: Each repetition of an experiment is called trial.' (Unit 8, Session 1)

questions.question: What is an outcome in probability?
options: The possible result of trials of an experiment
questions.explanation: The module: 'Outcome: The possible result of trials of an experiment is called outcome.' (Unit 8, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a sample space?
options: The set of all possible outcomes of an experiment
questions.explanation: The module: 'The sample space is the set of all possible outcomes of an experiment. It is denoted by the letter S.' (Unit 8, Session 1)

questions.question: What is an event in probability?
options: A subset of the sample space
questions.explanation: The module: 'An event is a subset of the sample space.' (Unit 8, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a simple event?
options: An event consisting of a single point of the sample space
questions.explanation: The module: 'Any event consisting of a single point of the sample space is known as a simple event in probability.' (Unit 8, Session 1)

questions.question: What is a compound event?
options: An event consisting of more than one single point of the sample space
questions.explanation: The module: 'If any event consists of more than one single point of the sample space then such an event is called a compound event.' (Unit 8, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the probability of an event E, P(E)?
questions.explanation: The module: 'P(E) = n(E) / n(S).' (Unit 8, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the range of probability values?
questions.explanation: The module: 'P(A) is always a nonnegative real number, between 0 and 1 inclusive.' (Unit 8, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the probability of an impossible event?
questions.explanation: The module: 'if A is the empty set, then P(A) = 0.' (Unit 8, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the probability of a certain event?
questions.explanation: The module: 'P(S) = 1, i.e., if A is the entire sample space S, then P(A)=1.' (Unit 8, Session 1)

questions.question: What are mutually exclusive events?
options: Events that cannot occur at the same time
Events that can occur at the same time
questions.explanation: The module: 'If the occurrence of one event excludes the occurrence of another event, such events are mutually exclusive events.' (Unit 8, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the addition rule for mutually exclusive events?
options: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)
questions.explanation: The module: 'For mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B).' (Unit 8, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the addition rule for non-mutually exclusive events?
options: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)
questions.explanation: The module: 'For non-mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B).' (Unit 8, Session 4)

questions.question: What are independent events?
options: Events where the occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the other
questions.explanation: The module: 'Independent Events: If the occurrence of any event is completely unaffected by the occurrence of any other event, such events are known as an independent event.' (Unit 8, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the multiplication rule for independent events?
questions.explanation: The module: 'When two events are independent, we can say that P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B).' (Unit 8, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the multiplication rule for dependent events?
questions.explanation: The module: 'For dependent events, P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B|A).' (Unit 8, Session 5)

questions.question: What is a complementary event?
options: An event that represents the remaining elements of the sample space not in the original event
An event that is the same as the original
An event that is mutually exclusive
questions.explanation: The module: 'For any event E1 there exists another event E1' which represents the remaining elements of the sample space S. E1' = S - E1.' (Unit 8, Session 5)

questions.question: What is the complement rule?
questions.explanation: The module: 'P(A') = 1 – P(A).' (Unit 8, Session 5)

questions.question: If P(A) = 0.3, what is P(A')?
What is the sample space for tossing a fair coin?
questions.explanation: The module: 'In flipping a coin the sample space(S) = {H,T}.' (Unit 8, Session 1)

questions.question: What is the sample space for tossing a die?
questions.explanation: The module: 'When a die is tossed, the sample space(S) = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}.' (Unit 8, Session 1)

questions.question: If two dice are rolled, what is the total number of outcomes?
questions.explanation: The module shows a table with 6x6=36 outcomes. (Unit 8, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the probability of getting a sum of 7 when two dice are rolled?
questions.explanation: Outcomes with sum 7: (1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1) – 6 outcomes, so probability = 6/36 = 1/6. (Unit 8, Session 2)

questions.question: If a card is drawn from a standard deck of 52 cards, what is the probability of drawing an ace?
questions.explanation: There are 4 aces in a deck, so probability = 4/52 = 1/13. (Unit 8, Session 2)

questions.question: If a card is drawn, what is the probability of drawing a red card?
questions.explanation: There are 26 red cards (hearts + diamonds), so probability = 26/52 = 1/2. (Unit 8, Session 2)

questions.question: If a card is drawn, what is the probability of drawing a black king?
questions.explanation: There are 2 black kings (spades and clubs), so probability = 2/52 = 1/26. (Unit 8, Session 4)

questions.question: If two events are mutually exclusive, what is P(A and B)?
questions.explanation: If events are mutually exclusive, they cannot occur together, so P(A and B) = 0. (Unit 8, Session 4)

questions.question: What is the probability of getting a head or a tail when tossing a fair coin?
questions.explanation: The events are mutually exclusive and exhaustive, so P(H or T) = P(H) + P(T) = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1. (Unit 8, Session 4)

questions.question: If P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.3, and A and B are independent, what is P(A and B)?
questions.explanation: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) = 0.4 × 0.3 = 0.12. (Unit 8, Session 5)

questions.question: If P(A) = 0.5, P(B) = 0.4, and P(A and B) = 0.2, what is P(A or B)?
questions.explanation: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B) = 0.5 + 0.4 - 0.2 = 0.7. (Unit 8, Session 4)

questions.question: What is a tree diagram used for in probability?
options: To show the possible outcomes of multiple events and their probabilities
To represent data in a graph
questions.explanation: The module uses a tree diagram to illustrate the probability of independent events like coin flips. (Unit 8, Session 5)

questions.question: What is experimental probability?
options: Probability based on the relative frequency of an outcome from an experiment
questions.explanation: The module: 'The experimental or estimated is given by P(E) = Number of times the outcome happens / Total number of times the experiment is done.' (Unit 8, Session 3)

questions.question: If a die is rolled 60 times and the number 4 appears 10 times, what is the experimental probability of getting a 4?
questions.explanation: Experimental probability = 10/60 = 1/6. (Unit 8, Session 3)

questions.question: What is the probability of getting an even number when a fair die is rolled?
questions.explanation: Even numbers: 2, 4, 6 – 3 outcomes, so probability = 3/6 = 1/2. (Unit 8, Session 2)

questions.question: If a card is drawn from a deck, what is the probability of drawing a heart?
questions.explanation: There are 13 hearts in a deck, so probability = 13/52 = 1/4. (Unit 8, Session 2)

questions.question: What is the complement of the event 'getting a 5' when rolling a die?
questions.explanation: The complement of {5} is all other outcomes: {1, 2, 3, 4, 6}. (Unit 8, Session 5)
